title: Allow mounting a file, not directory on tmpfs mount (or change --read-only behaviour)
body:
### Feature request description

A few applications I'm trying to containerize want to change /etc/resolv.conf inside container, and fail if they cannot. At the same time, I'm trying to harden containers as much as possible, and among other things I use --read-only for that, only leaving necessary volumes and tmpfs mounts writable.

When you use --read-only, /etc/resolv.conf is mounted read-only, and cannot be changed. I think this is the proper default behaviour, but there is no way around the error without removing --read-only.

### Suggest potential solution

I think the most flexible (and maybe easiest) solution is to allow mounting a file, not a directory on tmpfs mount. Why I think it's the easiest solution is because without --read-only /etc/resolv.conf is already mounted as a file on tmpfs, so I think there is support inside podman, it's just not exposed.

Changing --read-only behaviour will not be backwards-compatible and also I think the current behaviour is proper in default scenario using --read-only. Introducing a flag like --read-only-except-resolv-conf might be a solution, but it does not look appealing.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

The only alternative (without removing --read-only) I see is bind-mounting a host file at /etc/resolv.conf, because bind mounts support mounting a file, not directory. There are a few problems here - you have to prepopulate it manually on the host while tmpfs and volume mounts allow "copy" behaviour; also, you have to organize storing this file on the host somewhere and it does not land properly into any of the primitives like volume or something; moreover, container would have write access to a host file so you have to sanitize it after container had access to it before doing anything with it - when the file is ephemeral inside container, there is no such problem.

### Additional context

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
